followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: Elevated the 'only if' overclaiming from an implicit concern to an explicit moderate objection, consistent with codex's valid point. Added the underconstrained inference beyond ~4 Mpc as a formal objection. Maintained all cycle-1 objections. Slight upward nudge in reviewer_confidence (6→6.5) because codex independently reached a similar overall assessment (7.1 vs my 7.0, same 'promising' verdict). Did not raise overall score because gemini's high marks are not convincing given its failure to engage with the 'only if' issue, the base rate question, and the moderate novelty.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: Gemini's novelty score of 9/10 is unjustified. The BORG+Gadget-4 pipeline is explicitly the authors' own prior work (refs 9, 12). Novelty of application is moderate, not transformative.

Gemini's 'strong_select' verdict and 9/10 overall score do not engage seriously with the finite-box limitation (which Gemini rates 'minor' despite overlapping with the 10 Mpc claimed scale), the 'only if' overclaiming, or the lack of a base-rate comparison for c/a ≈ 0.24 in ΛCDM. These omissions make the review uncritical.

Gemini's technical soundness of 8 is inconsistent with having only one 'minor' objection. The codex and my reviews both identify multiple moderate objections that collectively warrant a lower technical soundness score.

Codex's claim that the 'only if' objection 'would_change_verdict_if_true' is overstated: even if the language is weakened to 'one consistent solution,' the paper's contribution (demonstrating the solution exists and quantifying it) remains meaningful and 'promising' is still appropriate.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: Codex: 'Only if' language is stronger than the inference demonstrates—the paper shows one posterior that works but does not exclude other non-spherical configurations. This is a genuine moderate objection I had implicitly but did not make explicit.

Codex: Inference beyond the directly constrained ~4 Mpc volume (i.e., the 5–10 Mpc sheet claims) relies substantially on the ΛCDM prior and posterior extrapolation, and the uncertainty in this regime is not separately quantified from in-constraint-region uncertainty.
Codex: The σ values (5.4σ, 3.5σ) reference posterior scatter across simulations, not an independent observational rejection of spherical symmetry—a legitimate statistical framing concern.
Gemini: The mock inference test (Extended Data Figs. 1–3) is a genuine methodological strength that meaningfully validates the kinematic origin of the sheet, and I agree it is well-executed.

main_claims.claim: A spherical infall model using the ensemble mean mass profile is 5.4σ below the ensemble mean flow at 1 Mpc and 3.5σ below at 2 Mpc, demonstrating spherical symmetry fails to reproduce the simulated dynamics.
main_claims.notes: Internally consistent comparison between the spherical collapse model applied to the ensemble's own M(r) and the mass-weighted radial velocity profile. The σ values reference posterior simulation-to-simulation scatter (169 realizations), not independent observational significance—a subtlety the paper does not flag. Within that framing, the result is sound.
main_claims.paper_location: Results section, Fig. 1 and surrounding text

main_claims.claim: The inferred mass distribution is strongly sheet-like with c/a = 0.24±0.08 in the 2–4 Mpc shell, aligned within ~13–18 deg of the Local Sheet north pole.
main_claims.notes: Reported across all 169 realizations; maximum c/a of 0.45 ensures no outlier drives the result. Alignment with the known Local Sheet lends physical credibility. The inference is, however, directly constrained only within ~4 Mpc; the sheet's extension to 10 Mpc relies substantially on the ΛCDM prior.

main_claims.claim: The sheet-like mass distribution is driven by observed tracer peculiar velocities rather than the anisotropic spatial distribution of the tracers, as shown by a mock inference using spherical model velocities.
main_claims.notes: Mock test design is sound: same tracer positions, velocities replaced by spherical model predictions with same M(r). The residual +z/−z asymmetry in the mock (attributed to M31's tangential velocity) is acknowledged but not fully quantified, slightly complicating the clean contrast with the real-data result.

main_claims.claim: The discrepancy between timing argument masses and Hubble flow estimates is reconcilable within ΛCDM 'only if' mass is strongly concentrated in a plane out to 10 Mpc.
main_claims.notes: The paper demonstrates one internally consistent solution (the inferred sheet) but does not perform model comparison against other non-spherical anisotropic geometries. 'Only if' overstates the constraint: the evidence supports 'in our posterior a sheet is required' rather than 'no other ΛCDM configuration can reconcile the data.' The extension to 10 Mpc is also beyond the directly constrained ~4 Mpc volume.

main_claims.claim: The peculiar velocity field is strongly anisotropic with polar infall ≳100 km/s out to 8 Mpc, a testable prediction currently unconstrained by data.
main_claims.notes: Physically well-motivated given the sheet geometry, but entirely a simulation extrapolation. No kinematic data exists from high-latitude directions within the constrained volume, so the posterior uncertainty in polar regions is dominated by the ΛCDM prior rather than observations.

main_claims.claim: The inferred Local Group mass is ΣM₂₀₀,c = 3.3±0.6×10¹² M⊙.
main_claims.notes: Substantially above recent dynamical estimates (refs 7, 8: 1.3–2.3×10¹² M⊙). The paper does not reconcile this discrepancy or test whether the higher mass is prior-driven. The value is self-consistent within the ensemble but creates unaddressed tension with other measurements.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The 35 km/s velocity scatter adopted for the 31 tracer galaxies accurately represents observational uncertainty and is not dominated by systematic errors such as distance indicator biases or Malmquist effects.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: This scatter term is the primary handle controlling how tightly the tracer velocities constrain the posterior mass geometry. If underestimated (e.g., due to distance ladder systematics), the inferred sheet would be artificially sharpened; if overestimated, the inference is less constraining than claimed.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The 169 HMC samples are sufficiently independent to treat ensemble statistics (means, percentiles) as representative of the true posterior. Independence criteria are deferred to ref. 12 without summary.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: HMC chains can exhibit high autocorrelation. If the effective sample size is substantially below 169, reported uncertainties (16th–84th percentiles) are underestimates and the mock-vs-real comparison is on shakier footing.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The ΛCDM prior (Planck 2018 parameters, specifically Ω_Λ) is correct. The cosmological constant directly affects the timing argument mass estimate and the spherical collapse model's predicted v(r).
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The entire reconciliation is conditional on ΛCDM. Modifications to Ω_Λ (e.g., from dynamical dark energy) would shift the predicted v(r) relation and alter the degree of tension that needs resolving.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The mass-weighted radial velocity profile (Eq. 1, summing all simulation particles) is a fair analog of the observed recession velocities of 31 luminosity-selected galaxy tracers, and selection effects in the tracer catalogue do not bias the comparison.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Galaxies are luminosity-selected, not mass-selected. If the 31 tracers preferentially lie in overdense or underdense environments within the sheet, the comparison with the mass-weighted mean velocity profile could be systematically offset.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the 5.4σ discrepancy computed using only the simulation-to-simulation scatter, and does it correctly propagate distance errors, velocity errors, and MW/M31 mass uncertainty in a combined sense? Could a fuller error budget reduce this significance?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The σ computation methodology is not detailed in the visible paper text. Reading the supplementary code (Supplementary Code 1) is required to determine whether observational uncertainties are folded into the denominator or only posterior scatter across realizations.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do the 169 selected HMC realizations satisfy a formal thinning or independence criterion, and what is the effective sample size? This determines whether reported 16th–84th percentile uncertainty ranges are reliable.
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This detail is deferred entirely to ref. 12 (Wempe et al. 2025, A&A 701), which is not included in the review bundle.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: How common is a c/a ≈ 0.24 mass environment around MW–M31-mass pairs in generic ΛCDM volumes (e.g., IllustrisTNG, EAGLE, Millennium)? This base rate is essential to evaluate whether 'consistent with ΛCDM' implies 'expected' or merely 'not excluded.'
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This comparison requires access to large-volume cosmological simulation halo catalogs and a careful mass-ratio and separation matching procedure not conducted in this paper. Ref. 19 (Neuzil et al. 2020) documents the unusual character of the observed Local Sheet but does not directly provide the ΛCDM c/a distribution for mass (rather than galaxy) distributions.

novelty_assessment: The core BORG + Gadget-4 constrained-simulation pipeline is directly from the authors' own prior publications (refs 9 and 12, Wempe 2024 and Wempe et al. 2025). The methodology is not novel to this paper. The new contribution is: (1) the specific finding that the pipeline requires a strongly sheet-like mass geometry to simultaneously satisfy the timing argument and the quiet Hubble flow; (2) quantitative characterization of that geometry (axis ratios, alignment with Local Sheet, polar void depths); (3) the mock inference validation showing the sheet is kinematically driven; and (4) the testable prediction of strong polar infall. The physical insight that non-spherical distributions relax the spherical infall tension is conceptually well-known, but has not been demonstrated quantitatively with constrained ΛCDM simulations of the actual Local Group environment. The novelty is therefore real but moderate—a new application and quantification of an existing framework rather than a methodological breakthrough. Gemini's rating of 9/10 for novelty is unjustified; a rating of 5–6/10 is more appropriate, reflecting that the finding is meaningful but built entirely on the authors' prior methodological work.
score_rationale:
Technical soundness: 7.0. The BORG+Gadget-4 pipeline is established and the 169-realization ensemble is reasonably large. The mock inference test is the paper's strongest methodological element. Deductions for: (a) the 40-Mpc box limitation overlapping with the 10-Mpc claimed scale; (b) all 31 tracers confined to low supergalactic latitude, leaving polar structure entirely prior-driven; (c) the 'only if' framing that overstates what the posterior comparison demonstrates; (d) unresolved tension between the inferred ΣM₂₀₀,c = 3.3×10¹² M⊙ and lower literature estimates.

Novelty: 5.5. The BORG+Gadget-4 methodology is the authors' own prior work. The new contribution is the geometric finding and its quantification—real but not a methodological breakthrough.

Significance: 7.5. Addresses a decades-old tension between timing argument masses and spherical infall estimates; produces a concrete, testable prediction (polar infall) that can be verified with future high-latitude dwarf galaxy surveys. Significance could rise if the base rate of this geometry in ΛCDM is shown to be non-negligible.

Clarity: 8.0. Well-organized for a Nature Astronomy letter format; the physical mechanism is explained clearly; the mock test is well-described. Minor deduction for not clarifying the statistical meaning of the quoted σ values.

Reviewer confidence: 6.5. Codex independently converged on a similar assessment (7.1, 'promising'), increasing confidence in the overall judgment. Key uncertainties remain: HMC sampling diagnostics (deferred to ref. 12), supplementary code (σ computation), and the ΛCDM base rate for the inferred geometry.
summary: Wempe et al. use 169 constrained ΛCDM resimulations (BORG Bayesian inference + Gadget-4) to resolve the long-standing tension between the timing argument lower bound on Local Group mass and spherical infall model estimates from the quiet Hubble flow. Their resolution is geometric: the mass distribution is strongly sheet-like (c/a ≈ 0.24 in the 2–4 Mpc shell), aligned with the Local Sheet and Supergalactic Plane, with deep polar voids. In a flattened geometry the Shell theorem does not apply, so off-axis mass partially offsets infall and explains the apparent Hubble flow quietness. A mock inference using spherical model velocities for the same tracer positions recovers no planar structure, validating that the inferred sheet is kinematically driven. The paper predicts strong polar infall (≳100 km/s within 8 Mpc) currently untestable due to the absence of known high-latitude tracers. After auditing cycle-1 reviews from codex and gemini, I maintain my cycle-1 assessment with refinements: the 'only if' overclaiming is a more serious objection than I initially rated, the inference beyond ~4 Mpc is more underconstrained than gemini acknowledges, and gemini's novelty-9 rating is unjustified given the methodology is from the authors' own prior publications.

technical_objections.evidence: No model comparison against alternative non-spherical geometries (e.g., filamentary configurations, different void placements) is performed. The Bayesian posterior, conditioned on ΛCDM prior and specific likelihood, produces a sheet—but this does not preclude other posterior families in different prior/likelihood configurations.
technical_objections.objection: The headline 'only if' language is stronger than the inference supports. The paper shows a sheet-like solution exists and works; it does not exclude other anisotropic non-spherical configurations that could also reconcile the tension.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract: 'but only if mass is strongly concentrated in a plane out to 10 Mpc'; Discussion: 'can be consistent … only if…'

technical_objections.evidence: Authors write: 'periodic boundary conditions produce anisotropic forces on scales of 10 Mpc or more, and they may bias the structure to align with the coordinate axes.' The coordinate misalignment relative to the observed Local Sheet (12 deg vs 5 deg from simulation z-axis) is a potential diagnostic of this artifact that is not fully addressed.
technical_objections.objection: The 40-Mpc simulation box with periodic boundary conditions produces acknowledged anisotropic tidal forces on scales ≳10 Mpc, overlapping with the claimed 10-Mpc sheet extent. The inferred sheet aligns better with the simulation coordinate axes than with the observed Local Sheet, consistent with a box artifact.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper itself states that strong anisotropy 'is not visible in our set of flow tracers because they all lie at low latitude in supergalactic coordinates.' The polar-region posterior scatter is reported to be larger (as expected from fewer constraints), but the quantitative contribution of the prior vs. data to polar structure is not separated.
technical_objections.objection: All 31 flow tracer galaxies lie at low supergalactic latitude. The posterior polar velocity field (strong infall ≳100 km/s) and polar void structure are extrapolations beyond the directly constrained region, yet their uncertainty is dominated by the ΛCDM prior rather than observations.

technical_objections.evidence: Ref. 19 (Neuzil et al. 2020) documents that the observed Local Sheet environment is unusual among ΛCDM halos. If a c/a ≈ 0.24 environment is rare (e.g., top 5% of ΛCDM realizations), the 'full consistency' framing misleads. The paper cites ref. 19's c/a = 0.16 for observed galaxies within 8 Mpc but does not compare to the ΛCDM distribution of c/a for similar mass pairs.
technical_objections.objection: The paper does not quantify how probable a c/a ≈ 0.24 sheet geometry is in unselected ΛCDM volumes near MW–M31-mass pairs. Without this base rate, 'full consistency within ΛCDM' conflates 'not excluded by ΛCDM' with 'expected by ΛCDM.'
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract ('demonstrating full consistency within the standard cosmological model'); Discussion

technical_objections.evidence: The σ values are computed as (ensemble mean flow − spherical model prediction) / (standard deviation of ensemble means). This measures internal consistency of the posterior, not an observational falsification of spherical symmetry. The framing is potentially misleading if read as 'spherical symmetry is excluded at 5.4σ.'
technical_objections.objection: The 5.4σ and 3.5σ discrepancy figures reference posterior simulation-to-simulation scatter, not independent observational rejection significance for a spherical mass distribution in nature. Treating them as evidential sigma values for the real Universe requires the posterior ensemble to be a faithful representation of uncertainty, which has not been independently validated in this paper.

technical_objections.evidence: Authors write: 'a significant asymmetry remains between the +z and −z directions … a quadrupolar matter distribution with the right orientation is visible about 8 Mpc from the centre.' This is a non-trivial residual attributed to M31 proper motion that is not formally accounted for in the mock comparison.
technical_objections.objection: The residual +z/−z asymmetry in the mock spherical inference (Extended Data Fig. 2) is acknowledged but not quantified. If M31's tangential velocity torque requirement can itself induce mild planar preference, the contrast between real and mock inferences that validates the sheet is slightly overstated.
